slides.title: print() basics
slides.markdown: `print()` sends text to the console. Strings go inside quotes.

slides.title: Variables
slides.markdown: Store a value in a name, then reuse it — or plug it into an f-string.

slides.title: Run your code
slides.markdown: Update the `name` value, press **Run**, and wait for the lesson to continue.

narration.script: Let's build a tiny hello world program in Python. Use print when you want Python to show a message in the terminal. Put the message inside quotes, then run it. Nice. Now make it personal with a variable named name. An f-string lets us place that name inside the message. Your turn: change the name, add one more print line, then run it and check the terminal.

cues.text: Build a tiny hello world program.
Use print to show a message.
Make the greeting personal with a variable.
Use an f-string to include the variable.
Pause, edit, and run your own version.

events.text: Let's build a tiny hello-world program in Python.
events.speech: Let's build a tiny hello world program in Python. I'll write the code quickly, but we'll pause long enough to understand what each line is doing.

events.text: Use print() when you want Python to show a message.
events.speech: We'll use the print function when we want Python to show something in the terminal. Put the message inside quotes, and Python will print it exactly as text.

files.main.py:
# My first Python program
print('Hello, RoadRunners!

events.text: That line is ready to run.
events.speech: That's the whole first line. The message is inside quotes, and print will send it to the terminal. Let's run it and see the output.

files.main.py:
# My first Python program
print('Hello, RoadRunners!')

events.text: Nice — now make it personal with a variable.
events.speech: Nice. Now let's make the greeting personal. A variable lets us store a value, like a name, and reuse it later.

events.text: An f-string can place the name inside the message.
events.speech: Now we'll use an f-string. It lets us drop the name variable directly into the message, so the output changes when the variable changes.

files.main.py:
# My first Python program
name = "learner"
print(f'

# My first Python program
name = "learner"
print(f'Hello, {name}!')

events.title: Make it yours
events.instructions: Change the name variable to your own name, then press Run to see your greeting in the terminal.

events.text: Your turn — edit the name or message, then hit Run.
events.speech: Your turn. Try changing the name, or add another print line. When you're ready, hit Run and check the terminal.

files.main.py:
# My first Python program
name = "learner"
print(f'Hello, {name}!')
print('Welcome to Python on RoadRunners')
